---
pagetitle: "ferx · open-source NLME for population PK"
toc: false
page-layout: full
---

::: {.hero-banner}
::: {.container}

::: {.hero-grid}
::: {.hero-left}

::: {.wordmark}
Fe[·]{.dot}[R[*x*]{style="font-style:italic"}]{.rx}
:::

### A nonlinear mixed-effects engine for *pharmacometrics*, written in Rust. {.headline}

::: {.lede}
ferx fits models defined in a small, readable DSL — bracket-sectioned `.ferx` files — powered by a Rust backend with [Enzyme](https://enzyme.mit.edu/) automatic differentiation for exact gradients. MIT-licensed.
:::

::: {.meta}
[**26** — iron, the element]{} &emsp; [**2026** — launch year]{} &emsp; [**MIT** — license]{}
:::

:::

::: {.hero-right}
::: {.sticker-stage-inline}
![](assets/ferx-hex-palette-c.svg){.sticker-sm}
:::

:::

:::
:::
:::

::: {.container style="padding: 3rem 1rem 1rem;"}
## How ferx is built

**ferx-core** is the Rust engine — it parses `.ferx` model files, runs the
estimators, computes gradients (Enzyme autodiff or finite differences), and
produces fits. All numerical work happens here. It ships with a standalone
command-line tool (the `ferx` CLI) that reads a `.ferx` file plus a CSV
dataset and writes results to disk, so you can use the engine directly with
no R involved.

**ferx-r** is a thin R wrapper around ferx-core. It exposes the same engine
through R functions, hands `data.frame`s to the core, and returns fit
objects you can pipe into R's plotting and reporting ecosystem. Because
ferx-core is a stable library, you (or anyone else) can build a different
wrapper on top of it — Python, Julia, a web service — and get the exact
same numerical results as the CLI and ferx-r.
:::

::: {.container style="padding: 3rem 1rem 2rem;"}
## Learn ferx

::: {.resource-grid style="display:grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(220px, 1fr)); gap: 1.25rem; margin-top: 1rem;"}

::: {.resource-card style="border: 1px solid var(--bs-border-color); border-radius: 8px; padding: 1.25rem;"}
**[ferx-r book](https://ferx-nlme.github.io/ferx-book/)**\
Comprehensive narrative guide — installation through ODE models, BLOQ, and IOV. All code runs on CI.
:::

::: {.resource-card style="border: 1px solid var(--bs-border-color); border-radius: 8px; padding: 1.25rem;"}
**[Get started](get-started.qmd)**\
Install ferx and run the warfarin quickstart in under five minutes.
:::

::: {.resource-card style="border: 1px solid var(--bs-border-color); border-radius: 8px; padding: 1.25rem;"}
**[Reference](reference/index.html)**\
Function-level documentation for every exported function in ferx-r.
:::

::: {.resource-card style="border: 1px solid var(--bs-border-color); border-radius: 8px; padding: 1.25rem;"}
**[Model DSL](model-dsl/overview.qmd)**\
Complete syntax reference for `.ferx` model files.
:::

:::
:::
